question_text: In humans, the sex of the child is determined by:
explanation: In humans, the male is heterogametic (XY) and produces two types of sperms — one carrying X chromosome and the other carrying Y chromosome. The female is homogametic (XX) and produces only X-bearing ova. When a sperm carrying Y chromosome fertilises the ovum, the child is male (XY); when a sperm carrying X chromosome fertilises the ovum, the child is female (XX). Hence, the father's chromosome determines the sex of the child.
subject: biology

options.text: The mitochondrial DNA of the mother
The autosomal chromosomes of both parents

elimination_hints.hint: The mother always contributes an X chromosome, so she cannot determine whether the child is male or female.
elimination_hints.misconception: Believing the mother determines the sex of the child

elimination_hints.hint: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited maternally but has no role in sex determination.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing mitochondrial inheritance with sex determination

elimination_hints.hint: Autosomal chromosomes (1–22) are not involved in sex determination; it is the sex chromosomes (X and Y) that matter.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing autosomes with sex chromosomes

question_text: Haemophilia is a sex-linked recessive disorder. A carrier woman marries a normal man. What is the probability that their sons will be haemophilic?
explanation: A carrier woman has the genotype X^H X^h (where X^h carries the haemophilia allele). A normal man has the genotype X^H Y. Their sons will receive Y from the father and either X^H or X^h from the mother. So 50% of sons will be X^h Y (haemophilic) and 50% will be X^H Y (normal). Therefore, the probability of a son being haemophilic is 50%.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: 100% would only be possible if the mother were homozygous affected (X^h X^h), not just a carrier.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming carrier means all offspring inherit the trait

elimination_hints.hint: 25% would be the proportion among ALL children (sons and daughters), not among sons specifically.
elimination_hints.misconception: Calculating probability among all offspring instead of sons only

elimination_hints.hint: 0% would apply only if the mother were homozygous normal (X^H X^H), not a carrier.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the carrier status of the mother

question_text: In the XX-XO type of sex determination seen in grasshoppers, the males have:
explanation: In the XX-XO system of sex determination found in grasshoppers, the females have two X chromosomes (XX) while the males have only one X chromosome (XO). Males produce two types of gametes — one with X and one without X (O). Females produce only X-bearing gametes. Males have one fewer chromosome than females.
subject: biology

options.text: One X chromosome and no Y chromosome
One X and one Y chromosome

elimination_hints.hint: Two X chromosomes (XX) represent the female genotype in the XX-XO system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the male and female karyotypes in the XX-XO system

elimination_hints.hint: There is no Y chromosome in the XX-XO system; this describes the XX-XY system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up XX-XO with XX-XY sex determination

elimination_hints.hint: Two Y chromosomes are not found in any standard sex determination system; moreover, Y chromosome does not exist in the XO system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding the XO system entirely

question_text: Which of the following is an example of a sex-linked recessive trait in humans?
explanation: Colour blindness is a classic example of an X-linked (sex-linked) recessive trait in humans. The gene for red-green colour blindness is located on the X chromosome. Males are more commonly affected because they have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive allele will express the phenotype.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Sickle-cell anaemia is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a mutation in the HBB gene on chromosome 11.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing autosomal recessive with sex-linked recessive

elimination_hints.hint: Phenylketonuria is an autosomal recessive metabolic disorder, not sex-linked.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly categorising autosomal traits as sex-linked

elimination_hints.hint: Thalassemia is an autosomal recessive blood disorder involving genes on chromosomes 11 and 16.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up blood-related disorders with sex-linked traits

question_text: In honey bees, the males (drones) develop from:
explanation: Honey bees exhibit a haplo-diploid sex determination system. The queen bee can lay two types of eggs — fertilised (diploid, 2n = 32) eggs that develop into females (queens or workers) and unfertilised (haploid, n = 16) eggs that develop into males (drones) by parthenogenesis. This means drones have no father but have a grandfather.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Fertilised diploid eggs give rise to females (queens or workers), not males.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the origin of males and females in honey bees

elimination_hints.hint: Unfertilised eggs are haploid, not diploid, because they have not undergone fusion with a sperm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming unfertilised eggs can be diploid

elimination_hints.hint: Fertilised eggs are always diploid because fertilisation restores the diploid chromosome number.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing fertilised with haploid condition

question_text: In birds, the sex determination system is ZW-ZZ. Which of the following statements is correct?
explanation: In birds, the female is heterogametic (ZW) and the male is homogametic (ZZ). This is the reverse of the XX-XY system in humans where the male is heterogametic. Therefore, in birds, the female determines the sex of the offspring because she produces two types of gametes — one carrying Z and the other carrying W.
subject: biology

options.text: Males are heterogametic with ZW chromosomes
Females are heterogametic with ZW chromosomes
Males are heterogametic with XY chromosomes
Females are homogametic with ZZ chromosomes

elimination_hints.hint: In the ZW-ZZ system, males are homogametic (ZZ), not heterogametic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the human XX-XY pattern to birds

elimination_hints.hint: Birds follow the ZW-ZZ system, not the XX-XY system. XY is found in humans and most mammals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the notation systems of different organisms

elimination_hints.hint: In birds, ZZ is the male genotype; females are ZW and therefore heterogametic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the sex chromosome constitution of males and females in birds

question_text: The pattern of inheritance where a trait passes from a carrier mother to her sons and from an affected father to his daughters (through carrier status) is known as:
explanation: Criss-cross inheritance is the characteristic pattern of X-linked genes where the trait appears to pass from the father to his grandsons through his carrier daughters. A father passes his X chromosome to all daughters (making them carriers if the allele is recessive) and his Y chromosome to all sons. A carrier mother passes the affected X to 50% of her sons who express the trait. This creates a criss-cross pattern across generations.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Autosomal inheritance involves genes on autosomes and does not show the characteristic father-to-daughter and mother-to-son pattern.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing autosomal with sex-linked patterns

elimination_hints.hint: Maternal inheritance refers to cytoplasmic (mitochondrial) inheritance, not to X-linked gene transmission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing maternal inheritance (mitochondrial) with X-linked inheritance

elimination_hints.hint: Polygenic inheritance involves multiple genes controlling a single trait (e.g., skin colour) and has nothing to do with sex-linked patterns.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up polygenic with sex-linked inheritance

question_text: A colour-blind man marries a woman who is a carrier for colour blindness. What proportion of their daughters will be colour blind?
explanation: The colour-blind man has genotype X^c Y and the carrier woman has genotype X^C X^c. The daughters receive one X from each parent. From the father they get X^c, and from the mother they get either X^C or X^c with equal probability. So daughters will be either X^C X^c (carrier, normal vision) or X^c X^c (colour blind). Therefore, 50% (1/2) of their daughters will be colour blind.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: All daughters would be colour blind only if the mother were also colour blind (X^c X^c), not merely a carrier.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming carrier mothers always pass the affected allele

elimination_hints.hint: The father always passes X^c to daughters, and the carrier mother passes X^c half the time, so some daughters will be affected.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the father's contribution of the affected X chromosome

elimination_hints.hint: One-fourth refers to the proportion among all children, not specifically among daughters.
elimination_hints.misconception: Calculating proportion among all offspring instead of among daughters only

question_text: Haemophilia is often referred to as the 'Royal disease' because:
explanation: Haemophilia is called the 'Royal disease' because Queen Victoria of England was a carrier of the haemophilia allele. She passed the gene to several of her children, who then married into various royal families across Europe, spreading the disorder among European royalty. The disease is X-linked recessive, so carrier females passed it to their sons.
subject: biology

options.text: Queen Victoria was a carrier and spread it to European royal families
It was first discovered in the royal palace of France
Only members of royal families can inherit this disease
The gene for haemophilia is present on the Y chromosome of royal males

elimination_hints.hint: The disease is associated with the British royal family and Queen Victoria, not the French royal palace.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect historical association of haemophilia

elimination_hints.hint: Haemophilia can occur in any family; it is not exclusive to royalty. The name 'Royal disease' is historical, not biological.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a historical nickname with biological exclusivity

elimination_hints.hint: Haemophilia is X-linked recessive, meaning the gene is on the X chromosome, not the Y chromosome.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing X-linked with Y-linked inheritance

question_text: Which of the following statements about the Barr body is correct?
explanation: A Barr body is a condensed, inactivated X chromosome found in the somatic cells of female mammals. According to the Lyon hypothesis, one of the two X chromosomes in each cell of a female is randomly inactivated during early embryonic development and appears as a darkly staining body at the periphery of the nucleus. This process ensures dosage compensation between males (one X) and females (two X chromosomes).
subject: biology

options.text: It is an extra Y chromosome in male cells
It is a condensed, inactivated X chromosome in female somatic cells
It is present in both male and female cells equally
It is the active X chromosome that controls gene expression

elimination_hints.hint: Barr bodies involve the X chromosome, not the Y chromosome, and are characteristic of female cells.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing X chromosome inactivation with Y chromosome behaviour

elimination_hints.hint: Normal males (XY) have only one X chromosome which remains active, so they do not have a Barr body. It is typically found only in female (XX) cells.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that Barr body formation requires two X chromosomes

elimination_hints.hint: The Barr body is the INACTIVE X chromosome; the other X chromosome remains active and controls gene expression.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the inactive X (Barr body) with the active X chromosome

question_text: A woman whose father was haemophilic marries a normal man. She is phenotypically normal. What are the chances that her son will be haemophilic?
explanation: Since the woman's father was haemophilic (X^h Y), he must have passed his X^h chromosome to all his daughters. The woman received X^h from her father and X^H from her normal mother, making her a carrier (X^H X^h). She is phenotypically normal because haemophilia is recessive. When she marries a normal man (X^H Y), her sons will receive either X^H or X^h from her, and Y from the father. So 50% of her sons will be haemophilic (X^h Y) and 50% will be normal (X^H Y).
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: 0% is incorrect because the woman is certainly a carrier (she received X^h from her haemophilic father), so she can pass the allele to her sons.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming a phenotypically normal woman cannot be a carrier

elimination_hints.hint: 25% would be the probability among all children, not specifically among sons.
elimination_hints.misconception: Calculating probability of haemophilia among all offspring instead of among sons only

elimination_hints.hint: 100% would require the mother to be homozygous affected (X^h X^h), but she is only a carrier (X^H X^h).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing carrier status with homozygous affected genotype

question_text: In Drosophila, sex is determined by the ratio of X chromosomes to sets of autosomes (X:A ratio). A fly with XXY chromosomes and 2 sets of autosomes (2A) would be:
explanation: In Drosophila, sex determination depends on the X:A ratio (genic balance theory), not the presence of Y chromosome as in humans. For a fly with XXY and 2A, the X:A ratio = 2X/2A = 1.0. An X:A ratio of 1.0 corresponds to a female. An X:A ratio of 0.5 corresponds to a male. This is different from humans where XXY produces a male (Klinefelter syndrome) because in humans the Y chromosome carries the SRY gene that determines maleness.
subject: biology

options.text: A male, similar to Klinefelter syndrome in humans
A super male due to the extra Y chromosome
A female, because the X:A ratio is 1.0

elimination_hints.hint: In humans, XXY produces Klinefelter males because the Y chromosome (SRY gene) determines maleness. But in Drosophila, Y has no role in sex determination.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying human sex determination logic to Drosophila

elimination_hints.hint: Intersex occurs in Drosophila when the X:A ratio is between 0.5 and 1.0 (e.g., 0.67). With X:A = 1.0, the fly is clearly female.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing intermediate X:A ratios with X:A = 1.0

elimination_hints.hint: In Drosophila, the Y chromosome does not determine maleness; the X:A ratio does. 'Super male' occurs at X:A = 0.33.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming Y chromosome determines maleness in Drosophila as in humans

question_text:
Assertion (A): Colour blindness is more common in males than in females.
Reason (R): The gene for colour blindness is located on the X chromosome and is recessive in nature.

Select the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is correct but R is incorrect
(D) A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are correct, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Colour blindness is X-linked recessive. Males have only one X chromosome (XY), so a single recessive allele on the X chromosome is sufficient to express colour blindness. Females have two X chromosomes (XX), so they need two copies of the recessive allele to be colour blind. This is why the trait is far more common in males — a single copy expresses in males (hemizygous) but females would need to be homozygous recessive.
subject: biology

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains WHY colour blindness is more common in males — because it is X-linked recessive and males are hemizygous for X.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the causal link between X-linked recessive nature and higher male prevalence

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct — the gene for colour blindness IS on the X chromosome and IS recessive.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly doubting the X-linked recessive nature of colour blindness

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is correct — colour blindness is indeed more common in males (approximately 8% males vs. 0.4% females).
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly believing colour blindness affects both sexes equally

question_text:
Assertion (A): Haemophilia is more commonly expressed in human males than in females.
Reason (R): The gene for haemophilia is located on the X chromosome, and a female carrier can pass the allele to her sons through criss-cross inheritance.

Select the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is correct but R is incorrect
(D) A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are factually correct. Haemophilia IS more common in males, and carrier females CAN pass the allele to sons (criss-cross inheritance). However, the reason does not correctly explain WHY haemophilia is more common in males. The actual explanation is that males are hemizygous (XY) — they have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive allele on X is sufficient for expression. The reason describes the transmission pattern (criss-cross inheritance from carrier mother to son) but does not explain the fundamental cause of higher male prevalence, which is hemizygosity. Hence, both are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A.
subject: biology

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: While both statements are true, the reason describes a transmission pattern (criss-cross inheritance), not the cause of higher male prevalence (hemizygosity).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the transmission mechanism with the reason for differential expression between sexes

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct — carrier females do pass the haemophilia allele to sons via criss-cross inheritance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly doubting the validity of criss-cross inheritance in haemophilia

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is correct — haemophilia is indeed far more common in males due to their hemizygous X chromosome.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly believing haemophilia affects both sexes equally

question_text:
Match the organisms in Column I with their sex determination mechanisms in Column II:

Column I:
(i) Humans
(ii) Birds
(iii) Grasshopper
(iv) Honey bee

Column II:
(p) ZW-ZZ system
(q) Haplo-diploid system
(r) XX-XY system
(s) XX-XO system

Select the correct match:
(A) (i)-r, (ii)-p, (iii)-s, (iv)-q
(B) (i)-p, (ii)-r, (iii)-q, (iv)-s
(C) (i)-r, (ii)-s, (iii)-p, (iv)-q
(D) (i)-s, (ii)-p, (iii)-r, (iv)-q
explanation: Humans follow the XX-XY system where males are heterogametic (XY) and females are homogametic (XX), so (i)-r. Birds follow the ZW-ZZ system where females are heterogametic (ZW) and males are homogametic (ZZ), so (ii)-p. Grasshoppers follow the XX-XO system where females are XX and males are XO (lacking a second sex chromosome), so (iii)-s. Honey bees follow the haplo-diploid system where males (drones) are haploid (from unfertilised eggs) and females are diploid (from fertilised eggs), so (iv)-q. The correct match is (A).
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Option B incorrectly assigns the ZW-ZZ system to humans and XX-XY to birds — this is the reverse of the actual situation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping sex determination systems between humans and birds

elimination_hints.hint: Option C incorrectly assigns XX-XO to birds and ZW-ZZ to grasshoppers — birds use ZW-ZZ and grasshoppers use XX-XO.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the sex determination systems of birds and grasshoppers

elimination_hints.hint: Option D assigns XX-XO to humans and XX-XY to grasshoppers, which is incorrect — humans use XX-XY and grasshoppers use XX-XO.
elimination_hints.misconception: Interchanging the sex determination systems of humans and grasshoppers

question_text:
Match the sex-linked conditions/features in Column I with their descriptions in Column II:

Column I:
(i) Haemophilia A
(ii) Colour blindness
(iii) Holandric genes
(iv) Barr body

Column II:
(p) Inability to distinguish red and green colours
(q) Condensed, inactivated X chromosome in female cells
(r) Genes located exclusively on the Y chromosome
(s) Deficiency of clotting factor VIII

Select the correct match:
(A) (i)-p, (ii)-s, (iii)-q, (iv)-r
(B) (i)-s, (ii)-r, (iii)-p, (iv)-q
(C) (i)-s, (ii)-p, (iii)-r, (iv)-q
(D) (i)-r, (ii)-p, (iii)-s, (iv)-q
explanation: Haemophilia A is caused by a deficiency of clotting factor VIII, so (i)-s. Colour blindness is the inability to distinguish red and green colours, so (ii)-p. Holandric genes are genes present exclusively on the Y chromosome (with no corresponding allele on X), so (iii)-r. The Barr body is a condensed, inactivated X chromosome found in female somatic cells, so (iv)-q. The correct match is (C).
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Option A swaps haemophilia A with colour blindness descriptions — haemophilia involves clotting factor deficiency, not colour vision.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the clinical features of haemophilia and colour blindness

elimination_hints.hint: Option B incorrectly assigns Y-linked genes to colour blindness and colour vision defect to holandric genes. Colour blindness is X-linked, not Y-linked.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing colour blindness gene on Y chromosome instead of X chromosome

elimination_hints.hint: Option D assigns Y-chromosome location to haemophilia A and clotting factor VIII deficiency to holandric genes — haemophilia A is X-linked, not Y-linked.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing X-linked haemophilia with Y-linked (holandric) genes

question_text: True or False: In humans, the father determines the sex of the child because he produces two types of sperms — one carrying the X chromosome and the other carrying the Y chromosome.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Human males are heterogametic (XY) and produce two types of sperms — 50% carry X chromosome and 50% carry Y chromosome. Females are homogametic (XX) and produce only X-bearing ova. If an X-bearing sperm fertilises the ovum, the resulting zygote is XX (female). If a Y-bearing sperm fertilises the ovum, the zygote is XY (male). Therefore, the sex of the child depends on whether the sperm carries X or Y, making the father the determinant of the child's sex.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is factually accurate as per NCERT — males produce X and Y bearing sperms, determining the sex of the child.
elimination_hints.misconception: Believing the mother determines the sex of the child

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for this question type.
This option is not applicable for this question type.

question_text: True or False: In the ZW-ZZ system of sex determination found in birds, the male is heterogametic.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. In the ZW-ZZ system found in birds, the FEMALE is heterogametic (ZW) and produces two types of gametes (Z-bearing and W-bearing), while the MALE is homogametic (ZZ) and produces only Z-bearing gametes. This is the opposite of the human XX-XY system where the male is heterogametic.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: In birds, the female (ZW) is heterogametic, not the male (ZZ). This is the reverse of the human system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying human XX-XY pattern to birds where males are heterogametic

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for this question type.
This option is not applicable for this question type.

question_text: Genes that are located exclusively on the Y chromosome and are passed only from father to son are called __________ genes.
explanation: Genes that are present exclusively on the non-homologous region of the Y chromosome (with no corresponding allele on the X chromosome) are called holandric genes. These genes are inherited strictly through the male line — from father to all sons — because only males possess the Y chromosome. Examples include the SRY gene (sex-determining region Y) and genes for hypertrichosis (excessive hair growth on the ear pinna). Females never inherit these genes as they lack the Y chromosome.
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: Autosomal genes are located on autosomes (non-sex chromosomes), not on the Y chromosome.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Y-linked genes with autosomal genes

elimination_hints.hint: Epistatic refers to the interaction between genes at different loci where one gene masks the effect of another. It has nothing to do with chromosome location.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing gene interaction terms with gene location terms

elimination_hints.hint: Pleiotropic refers to a single gene affecting multiple phenotypic traits. It describes gene function, not gene location on a specific chromosome.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing gene effect terminology with chromosomal location

question_text: A genetics counsellor analyses the pedigree of a family. The grandfather (maternal) was colour blind. The grandmother had normal vision and was not a carrier. Their daughter married a man with normal colour vision. What is the probability that the couple's first son will be colour blind?
explanation: The maternal grandfather is colour blind, so his genotype is X^c Y. The grandmother has normal vision and is not a carrier, so her genotype is X^C X^C. Their daughter receives X^c from the father and X^C from the mother, making her a carrier (X^C X^c) with normal vision. She marries a normal man (X^C Y). For their sons: sons receive Y from the father and either X^C or X^c from the carrier mother. So 50% of sons will be X^c Y (colour blind) and 50% will be X^C Y (normal). The probability that the first son will be colour blind is 50% (1/2).
subject: biology

elimination_hints.hint: The daughter is obligatorily a carrier because she received X^c from her colour-blind father. She CAN pass it to her sons.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring that the daughter must be a carrier if her father was affected

elimination_hints.hint: 25% would be the probability among all children (sons and daughters combined), but the question asks specifically about the son.
elimination_hints.misconception: Calculating probability among all offspring rather than among sons

elimination_hints.hint: 100% would only occur if the mother were homozygous colour blind (X^c X^c). Here she is a carrier (X^C X^c), so only half her sons will be affected.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing carrier status with homozygous affected condition
